coredata.dc:title: Electronic and photocatalytic properties of modified MoS2/graphene quantum dots heterostructures: A computational study
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ning
Liu, Zhengtang
Hu, Shengliang
Chang, Qing
Xue, Chaorui
Wang, Huiqi
coredata.dc:description: Solar energy absorption and photo-induced charge separation and transfer are crucial to enhance photocatalytic properties. Here, the structural, electronic and photocatalytic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), pure and O, N, Sv-modified MoS2 monolayers, GQDs-based heterostructures have been studied by theoretical calculations based on density functional theory. Compared with the GQDs, the obvious red shifts of the absorption peaks can be observed, the absorption intensities increases evidently, and the typical type-II band alignments can be formed after the construction of the pure, O, N, Sv-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures, which is beneficial to promote photo-induced charge transfer and more visible light harvesting. Subsequently, it is worth mentioning that N-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure also has the lower binding energy and higher absorption in the infrared region and impactful photo-induced electron injection from GQDs to N-MoS2 surface. Therefore, this work provides an instrumental and promising approach in designing new GQDs-based heterostructures to enhance the solar energy absorption and conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Modified MoS2/GQDs heterostructures
Density functional theory calculations
Type-II band alignments
Light harvesting
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic configurations of GQDs (a), D-modified MoS2 monolayers (b) and T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures (c and d).
(a) The formation energy of the O, N, Sv-MoS2 monolayers; (b) The binding energy of T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures at different h preset or h AO.
Partial density of states (PDOS) of the GQDs, the inset in the figure shows the energy level structure of the GQDs.
Partial density of states (PDOS) of pure and O, N, Sv-MoS2 monolayers, the insets in these figure are the corresponding band structures.
Partial density of states (PDOS) of T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures, the insets in these figure are the corresponding band structures.
Optical absorption (a), the calculated VBM, CBM and other level positions of GQDs, pure and O, N, Sv-modified MoS2 monolayers (b), T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures (c).
Plane-average charge density difference along the z-direction of T-MoS2/GQDs interfaces. The insertion represents (a) MoS2/GQDs, (b) O-MoS2/GQDs, (c) N-MoS2/GQDs, (d) Sv-MoS2/GQDs, respectively.

head.ce:title: Electronic and photocatalytic properties of modified MoS2/graphene quantum dots heterostructures: A computational study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Solidification Processing, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Solidification Processing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar energy absorption and photo-induced charge separation and transfer are crucial to enhance photocatalytic properties. Here, the structural, electronic and photocatalytic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), pure and O, N, Sv-modified MoS2 monolayers, GQDs-based heterostructures have been studied by theoretical calculations based on density functional theory. Compared with the GQDs, the obvious red shifts of the absorption peaks can be observed, the absorption intensities increases evidently, and the typical type-II band alignments can be formed after the construction of the pure, O, N, Sv-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures, which is beneficial to promote photo-induced charge transfer and more visible light harvesting. Subsequently, it is worth mentioning that N-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure also has the lower binding energy and higher absorption in the infrared region and impactful photo-induced electron injection from GQDs to N-MoS2 surface. Therefore, this work provides an instrumental and promising approach in designing new GQDs-based heterostructures to enhance the solar energy absorption and conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a low cost and green material, have tremendous potential in the applications of solar-to-energy conversion, fluorescence imaging and sensing owing to their excellent performances in such as photoluminescence, photostability, biocompatibility and non-toxicity [1–6]. However, CDs also have two challenges in the difficulty of photo-induced charge transfer and the lower visible light absorption because the CDs have high charge recombination probability and strong absorption in ultraviolet region [3,5,6]. Therefore, in order to promote the photo-induced charge-transfer and capture more visible light, CD-based heterostructures and hybrid systems have been built. The CDs-combined compounds include noble metals (Ag, Au, Pt), TiO2, Ag3PO4, ZnFeO4, Fe2O3, C3N4, Ni3S2, Bi2WO6, BiOCl, Cu2O, Cu1.8S and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) [1–5,7–13], which exhibits the better efficiencies of visible light-harvesting and charge-transfer.

In these CDs-based compounds, two dimensional (2D)-TMDs have the advantages of huge specific surface area, strong visible light absorption and short migration distance of photo-generated charge carrier [14]. Atkin et al. [13] reported the CDs/2D WS2 hybrids with enhanced photocatalytic activity for Congo red (CR) dye. Fang et al. [2] demonstrated the effective transfer of photo-excited electrons from the CDs to the MoS2 monolayers. Nevertheless, it is generally known that the edge sites of 2D MoS2 or WS2 are catalytically active yet the basal planes are inert [15–20], which restricts the solar-energy conversion. Thus several solutions based on experiments (plasma exposure [17], hydrogen etching [18], etc.) or calculations (structural defect [19], doping [20], etc.) have been implemented by researchers. For instance, oxygen-incorporated MoS2 may provide more defects leading to more catalytically active sites [16]. The inert basal planes can be activated via the synergistic effects of nitrogen and fluorine co-doping [20]. Consequently, as previously mentioned, it is significant to construct CDs-based heterostructures by activated 2D TMDs and investigate the interfacial interactions for designing excellent performance.

Density-functional theory (DFT) calculations become a powerful approach to research the intrinsic properties of nanomaterial and provide theoretical support for the potential applications. For 2D materials, dispersion corrections can’t be well described by common generalized gradient approximation (GGA) exchange-correlation functional. One solution is to add the standard Kohn–Sham-DFT energies with semi-empirical dispersion correction (D), resulting in the DFT-D approach [21–25]. Thus, as one kind of CDs [1,26], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are chosen to couple with O, N and S-vacancy modified MoS2 (O, N, Sv-MoS2) monolayer in van der Waals interactions based on this study. Then structural, electronic and photocatalytic properties of modified MoS2/GQDs heterostructures are investigated by DFT-D approach.

The geometry optimizations and properties calculations were performed utilizing the CASTEP code with ultrasoft pseudopotential method on the basis of DFT [19–25,27]. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [19–25,27] and long-range dispersion corrections proposed by the Grimme (D) [21,22] were employed (Table S1† shows C 6 Parameters (in Jnm6 mol−1) and van der Waals Radii R 0 (in Å) for Elements). Valence electron configurations include Mo4d4 5s2, S 3s2 3p4, O 2s2 2p4, N 2s2 2p3, C 2s2 2p2 and H 1s1 electrons. The k point of GQDs was gamma point, and the k-point mesh generated by the Monkhorst-Pack of other models was adopted 0.015 Å−1 separation in brillouin zone during the relaxation and electronic calculation periods. The cutoff energy for the plane-wave basis set was 500 eV. All the geometry structures were fully relaxed until the force on each atom was less than 0.01 eV Å−1, and the convergence criterion of energy was 10−5 eV. Finally, the large vacuum regions (15 Å along c direction between isolated MoS2 monolayers or GQDs/MoS2 heterostructures, and beyond 10 Å in all three directions for GQDs) were added to avoid spurious interaction.

Firstly, all edge carbon atoms of GQDs were assumed to be fully passivated by H atoms to eliminate the dangling bonds [28] and a zigzag-edged C24H12 structure was constructed (Fig. 1 (a)). Displayed in Fig. 1(b), 7 × 7 × 1 supercells containing 147 atoms for pure, O and N-modified MoS2 or 146 atoms for Sv-modified MoS2 were built (D-MoS2 and T-MoS2 refer to the dopant-modified MoS2 and total MoS2 monolayers, respectively). After geometry optimization, the lattice constant of MoS2 monolayer were a = b = 3.163 Å and α = β = 90°, γ = 120°, which is in agreement with the published data [23,29–31]. The formation energy (E f) of the O, N, Sv-modified MoS2 can be calculated by the reported expression [23,27,30]. E f values of O-MoS2 and N-MoS2 monolayers are negative and E f values of Sv-MoS2 monolayers are positive (Fig. 2 (a)), indicating that the O and N dopants prefer to substitute the S atoms and these doping systems are relatively stable. After that, T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures are built, as displayed in Fig. 1(c) and (d). The distance between T-MoS2 monolayer and GQDs is h (h preset represents the values of default setting, and h AO refers to the optimized values). In order to accurately study the electronic and photocatalytic properties of the T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures, it needs to find the most stable T-MoS2/GQDs interface. Thus the binding energies (E b) of T-MoS2/GQDs interfaces are calculated by the following expression [25,29,32]:
(1)
E b
=
E
T - MoS2 / GQDs
-
E MoS2
-
E GQDs
-
(
μ D
-
μ S
)
where E T-MoS2/GQDs, E MoS2 and E GQDs are the total energies of the T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures, MoS2 monolayer and GQDs, respectively. μ S and μ D are the chemical potentials of the substituted atoms (S) and the dopant atoms, respectively. In order to obtain the most stable heterostructure, the positions of defect in the heterostructures are test. Fig. 2(b) shows the E b of the most stable MoS2/GQDs interface at different h preset and h AO values. It can be seen that the optimized h AO values range from 2.96 Å to 3.50 Å, among which the optimal h AO is 3.19 Å. Meanwhile, the lowest E b is −1.24 eV, indicating that the MoS2/GQDs heterostructure reaches the most stable state at the distance of 3.19 Å. After taking the same optimization method, the best E b values of D-MoS2/GQDs interfaces at the optimal h preset and h AO value are shown in the inset of Fig. 2(b). As seen, O or N-MoS2/GQDs are steadier than the pure MoS2/GQDs sample, yet the Sv-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure is unstable due to the positive binding energy.

Fig. 3 displays the partial density of states (PDOS) of the GQDs, and the inset of this figure shows the energy level structure of the GQDs. The valence bands from −5 eV to −2 eV are mainly hybridized from C 2p and H 1 s orbitals, while the valence bands from −2 eV to 0 eV and the conduction bands from −2 to 3 eV originate predominantly from C 2p orbital (Fig. 3). Moreover, the energy levels of the GQDs is discrete owing to the quantum confinement [28] and the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) (E g HL) is 2.870 eV, which is in reasonable agreement with result (2.9 eV [28]) calculated by DFT-LDA. The calculated (based on different methods) and experimental energy gap values of GQDs are shown in Table S2† [28,33–35]. As seen, the energy gap values are diverse owing to different calculation methods. Moreover, the calculated energy gaps are lower than the experimental result, which attributes to the fact that the most commonly used local and semi-local approximations for exchange-correlation functional in standard DFT tend to underestimate the band gaps [21] . So the energy gaps between calculation and experiment exist the qualitative comparison relation.

Fig. 4 (a)–(d) show the PDOS of the pure and D-MoS2 monolayers, and the corresponding insets of these figures are the band structures of the pure and D (O, N, Sv)-MoS2 monolayers. Displayed in Fig. 4(a), the valence bands and the conduction bands are essentially hybridized from Mo 4d and S 3p orbitals. The direct band gap (both the conduction band minimum (CBM) and the valence band maximum (VBM) are located at the K symmetry point) of perfect MoS2 monolayer is 1.74 eV (Fig. 4(a) inset), which is consistent with DFT-GGA (1.78 eV [29–31], 1.70 eV [36]), lower than GW method (2.07 eV [37]) and concordant with experimental results (1.75 eV [38], 1.74 eV [39], 1.80 eV [40]) (Table S2†). When the MoS2 monolayer is doped with O atom, it can be seen in Fig. 4(b) that the VBM and CBM are both at the K symmetry points, and they dominantly consist of the hybridization from the Mo 4d, S 3pand O 2p orbitals. Though the VBM and CBM positons of the N-MoS2 are in according with the O-MoS2 monolayers, there is defect level (near the VBM) in the forbidden band (Fig. 4(c)), indicating the acceptor doping. The defect level is essentially are basically hybridized from N 2p, S 2p and Mo 4d orbitals. Moreover, the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO (Eg HL ) is 1.35 eV. For the Sv-MoS2 monolayer shown in Fig. 4(d), the defect levels with Mo 4d and S 3p hybrid orbitals are located at ∼1.22 eV (near the conduction band), suggesting the donor doping. Meanwhile, the VBM occurs at the G symmetry point and Eg HL value is 1.22 eV.

The PDOS and band structure of the T-MoS2 (pure and D-MoS2)/GQDs heterostructures are illustrated in Fig. 5 (a)–(d) and the insets. As seen, there are energy levels below the Fermi level. For the pure, O, Sv-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures, these energy levels are mainly composed of C 2p orbital. Yet that energy level (near −0.01 eV) of the N-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure is hybridized from C 2p, N 2p, Mo 3d and S 3p orbitals. In addition, the defect level at 0.87 eV appears near the conduction band of Sv-MoS2/GQDs, which originates predominantly from Mo 4d and S 3p hybrid orbitals. The VBM and CBM positions of the pure, O, N, Sv-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures are situated at −0.39 eV, −0.42 eV, −0.42 eV, −0.34 eV and 1.35 eV, 1.28 eV, 1.30 eV, 1.35 eV, respectively (the insets of Fig. 5). Eg HL values of these samples are 1.35 eV, 1.28 eV, 1.30 eV and 0.87 eV, respectively.

It is necessary to enhance the solar absorption for heightening the photocatalytic activity [1–5]. The absorption spectra are calculated from the dielectric function [29,41–45]:
(2)
α
=
(
2
ω
)
[
ε 1
( ω )
2
+
ε 2
( ω )
2

ε 1
( ω )
]
1 2
where ω is the light frequency,
ε 2
( ω )
and
ε 1
( ω )
are the imaginary and real parts of the dielectric function. The
ε 1
( ω )
is determined by summation over electronic states using the equation [41], and the The
ε 2
( ω )
is obtained using the Kramers-Kronig relationship [41]:
(3)
ε 1
( ω )
=
1 +
2 π
P
∫ 0 ∞
ε 2
(

)

ω 2
+ i η
d ω ′

The calculated
ε 1
( ω )
and
ε 2
( ω )
of dielectric function versus energy of T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures are displayed in Fig. S1†. The absorption peaks of GQDs are mainly distributed in the ultraviolet region (>∼3.10 eV) and the absorption intensity is relatively low (Fig. 6 (a)), which is not conducive to highly-efficient photocatalysis. By contrast, there is strong absorption in the range of ∼1.50–6.50 eV for the MoS2 monolayers, and the peaks at ∼3.10 eV and ∼6.20 eV are mainly from the transition between Mo 4d and S 3p orbitals. The obvious red shifts of the absorption peaks can be observed and the absorption intensities increases evidently after the construction of the T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures (the position, intensity and origin of the corresponding peaks A and B are summarized in Table S3† (supplemental materials)). The peak intensity at ∼2.48 eV of Sv-MoS2/GQDs is lower than other three heterostructures. As displayed in the inset of Fig. 6(a), it is worthy to be mentioned that the N-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure has higher absorption in the infrared region (∼0.45–1.24 eV), which can be considered as an efficient strategy to design the GQDs-based heterojunction composites for enhancing visible-infrared light photocatalysis.

So far, the appropriate energy gap and strong absorption of the T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures have been discussed in the above analysis. Furthermore, the suitable band edge is momentous for enhancing the photocatalytic activity. Here the redox ability has been investigated through the positions of the VBM and CBM relative to the vacuum energy level. These positions can be given by the following expression [36]:
(5)
E VB
= φ = V
( ∞ )
-
E F
(6)
E CB
=
E VB
-
E g
where V(∞), E F, E g are the electrostatic potential in a vacuum region, the Fermi level of the neutral surface system and the band gap, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the LUMO and HOMO of GQDs locates at higher energy states than the CBM and VBM of T-MoS2 monolayers, respectively. Thus it will form the typical type-II band alignments [1–4,32], which facilitates photo-induced charge separation and transfer. Combining with the detailed analysis on the PDOS and band structures, the photo-generated electron of C 2p orbital of pure, O and Sv-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures will finally transfer to the S 3p, O 2p (only for O-MoS2/GQDs) and Mo 4d hybrid orbitals (Figs. 4 and 6(c)). As for N-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure, the hybridization of C 2p, N 2p, Mo 3d and S 3p orbitals at −0.01 eV suggests the strong wave function overlap, resulting in the impactful photo-induced electron injection from GQDs to N-MoS2 surface [32]. In particular, it deserves to be mentioned that the defect levels (like S vacancy) in the N and Sv-MoS2 not only increase the active sites, but also efficiently boost photo-induced charge separation and transportation [46].

The innate character of the charge transfer in the T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures can be elucidated by the interfacial charge density difference. Figs. S2† and 7 show the 3D charge density difference and the plane-averaged charge density difference Δρ(z) along the z-direction, respectively. The plane-averaged charge density difference can be obtained as [42–44]:
(7)
Δ ρ
=
ρ
T - M o
S 2
/ G Q D s
-
ρ
T - M o
S 2
-
ρ
GQDs
where
ρ
T - M o
S 2
/ G Q D s
is the plane-averaged density of the T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure,
ρ
T - M o
S 2
and
ρ
GQDs
are the plane-averaged densities of the free-standing pure, doped MoS2 monolayer and GQDs, respectively. As shown in Figs. S2† and 7, electrons mainly accumulate in T-MoS2 interface and holes mainly accumulate in GQDs interface, which obviously displays that the charge transfer from GQDs to T-MoS2 [43,44]. Especially, the charge aggregation and depletion peaks in the interface region of N-MoS2 and GQDs increase significantly in contrast to other three samples, indicating the increment of the interfacial interaction and the magnitude of the charge transfer [29,42]. Moreover, the detailed charge accumulation and depletion of T-MoS2/GQDs interfaces according to Mulliken analysis (Fig. S3†) are consistent with the above discussion. Therefore, from the above analysis, it can be concluded that the construction of the GQDs-based heterostructures through activated MoS2 monolayers is propitious to the improvement of the solar energy absorption and conversion.

In summary, effects of different dopants in the MoS2 monolayer on the structural, electronic and photocatalytic properties of GQDs, pure and O, N, Sv-modified MoS2 monolayers, T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures are comprehensively studied. First, the negative formation energy (−1.94 eV and −2.14 eV, respectively) for O or N-MoS2 monolayers and binding energy (−3.48 eV and −3.56 eV, respectively) for O or N-MoS2/GQDs illustrate that the O and N dopants prefer to substitute the S atoms and these structural models are correspondingly stable. Second, there are defect levels in the forbidden bands of N and Sv-MoS2 monolayer, and the difference is that the former is acceptor-like level and the latter is donor-like level. Meanwhile, the energy levels which originate predominantly from C 2p orbitals (the hybridization of C 2p, N 2p, Mo 3d and S 3p orbitals for N-MoS2/GQDs) are located just below the Fermi levels of the T-MoS2/GQDs heterostructures. Importantly compared with the GQDs, the obvious red shifts of the absorption peaks can be observed, the absorption intensities increases evidently, and the typical type-II band alignments can be formed after the construction of the T-oS2/GQDs heterostructures, which is beneficial to promote photo-induced charge-transfer and capture more visible light. Finally, it is worthy to be mentioned that the N-MoS2/GQDs heterostructure also has high absorption in the infrared region and impactful photo-induced electron injection from GQDs to N-MoS2 surface.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51272301, 51502270, 21703209), the fund of the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing in NWPU (SKLSP201719), the authors also appreciate the financial support by the Shanxi Province Science Foundation (201601D021059), Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (201701D221087), the Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, North University of China Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars and “333” talent project research, the work rewards of excellent Ph.D. graduates come to Jin (2017) and North University of China Fund for Scientific Innovation Team.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.12.122.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Oxygen-incorporated MoS2 nanosheets with expanded interlayers for hydrogen evolution reaction and pseudocapacitor applications

Tunable doping in hydrogenated single layered molybdenum disulfide
Activating inert basal planes of MoS2 for hydrogen evolution reaction through the formation of different intrinsic defects
